questions.stem: A 22-year-old male presents with a three-week history of cough with blood-streaked sputum, macroscopic haematuria, and oliguria. Blood pressure is 160/95 mmHg. Serum creatinine is 600 µmol/L. Urine dipstick shows blood +++ and protein ++. Chest X-ray reveals bilateral patchy infiltrates. Anti-GBM antibodies are positive. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of pulmonary haemorrhage (haemoptysis, bilateral infiltrates) and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (haematuria, proteinuria, acute kidney injury) with positive anti-GBM antibodies is classic for Goodpasture syndrome. The other vasculitides (GPA, MPA) are ANCA-associated and typically ANCA-positive, not anti-GBM positive.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman is found to have microscopic haematuria and subnephrotic proteinuria on routine examination. Over two weeks, serum creatinine rises from 80 to 300 µmol/L. She has no respiratory symptoms. Anti-GBM antibodies are positive; ANCA and ANA are negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Anti-GBM disease can present with renal involvement alone (renal-limited) without pulmonary haemorrhage. The rapid rise in creatinine and positive anti-GBM antibodies exclude IgA nephropathy, thin basement membrane disease, FSGS, and membranous nephropathy, which do not have anti-GBM antibodies.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with massive haemoptysis, acute dyspnoea, and anuria. He requires intubation and ventilation. Serum creatinine is 900 µmol/L. Urine dipstick shows blood +++ and protein +. Chest X-ray demonstrates diffuse alveolar haemorrhage. Anti-GBM antibodies are positive. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The acute presentation of both life-threatening pulmonary haemorrhage and rapidly progressive renal failure with positive anti-GBM is diagnostic of Goodpasture syndrome. Pulmonary embolism and interstitial nephritis do not cause this combination. GPA and MPA are ANCA-associated and would not have anti-GBM antibodies.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with previously diagnosed Goodpasture syndrome, treated with cyclophosphamide and steroids and in remission, presents with sudden haemoptysis and oliguria. Anti-GBM antibody titres are again elevated. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The recurrence of symptoms with rising anti-GBM antibodies indicates a relapse of Goodpasture syndrome. Other conditions like GPA or lupus would have different serology (ANCA, ANA). Pneumonia and PE are not associated with elevated anti-GBM titres.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with three months of polyarthralgia, myalgia, and low-grade fever. She then develops haemoptysis and oliguria. Serum creatinine is 400 µmol/L. Urine dipstick shows blood ++ and protein +. Anti-GBM antibodies are positive. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Although the prodrome of arthralgia and myalgia is atypical, the subsequent development of pulmonary-renal syndrome with positive anti-GBM antibodies confirms Goodpasture syndrome. Rheumatoid arthritis and polymyalgia do not cause glomerulonephritis or haemoptysis. GPA and MPA are ANCA-positive, not anti-GBM.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old male smoker presents with haemoptysis, macroscopic haematuria, and oliguria. BP 160/95 mmHg, serum creatinine 250 µmol/L. Which single investigation is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Anti-GBM antibodies are specific for Goodpasture syndrome; ANCA is typically associated with vasculitis, ANA with lupus, CXR is non-specific for glomerulonephritis, and renal ultrasound does not diagnose the underlying pathology.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (creatinine 500 µmol/L) and pulmonary haemorrhage. Serum anti-GBM antibodies are equivocal on initial testing. Which investigation is definitive for diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Renal biopsy with immunofluorescence showing linear IgG deposition along the GBM is pathognomonic for anti-GBM disease, even when serology is equivocal. Other options are less specific or confirmatory.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman is found to have microscopic haematuria and mild proteinuria (0.5 g/day) on routine testing. Renal function is normal and she has no haemoptysis. Serum anti-GBM antibodies are negative. What is the most appropriate next step to confirm or exclude anti-GBM disease?
questions.explanation: Up to 10% of anti-GBM disease is seronegative; renal biopsy with immunofluorescence is the gold standard and can demonstrate linear IgG staining even when serum antibodies are undetectable.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents with haemoptysis, haematuria and serum creatinine 300 µmol/L. ANCA is positive (anti-MPO) and anti-GBM antibodies are negative. Which investigation is most helpful to differentiate ANCA-associated vasculitis from anti-GBM disease?
options: Repeat ANCA and anti-GBM in 2 weeks
questions.explanation: Renal biopsy with immunofluorescence distinguishes the two: anti-GBM disease shows linear IgG staining, while ANCA vasculitis shows pauci-immune staining (no or scant immune deposits). The other tests cannot reliably differentiate.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known anti-GBM disease (diagnosed 6 months ago, currently in remission on low-dose steroids) presents with macroscopic haematuria and a rise in serum creatinine from 90 to 160 µmol/L. Serum anti-GBM antibodies are negative. Which investigation is most likely to reveal the cause of his deterioration?
questions.explanation: In a patient with prior anti-GBM disease, recurrence can be seronegative. Renal biopsy can confirm relapse (linear IgG) or identify another pathology (e.g., de novo ANCA vasculitis). Ultrasound would exclude obstruction but not the glomerular cause.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man presents with haemoptysis, oliguria, and rapidly rising creatinine (800 μmol/L). Anti-GBM antibodies are positive. He is started on haemodialysis. Which of the following is the most important first-line therapy?
questions.explanation: In severe anti-GBM disease with pulmonary haemorrhage and dialysis-dependence, urgent plasma exchange is indicated to rapidly remove pathogenic anti-GBM antibodies. Immunosuppression (corticosteroids and cyclophosphamide) is given concurrently, but plasma exchange is the definitive life-saving intervention.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with haematuria and mild haemoptysis. Renal function is normal (creatinine 80 μmol/L). Renal biopsy shows crescentic glomerulonephritis with linear IgG staining; anti-GBM antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most appropriate first-line immunosuppressant?
questions.explanation: In anti-GBM disease, cyclophosphamide is the first-line cytotoxic agent for induction of remission, given with high-dose corticosteroids. Mycophenolate and azathioprine are second-line; rituximab is reserved for refractory cases.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with anti-GBM disease is receiving oral cyclophosphamide (2 mg/kg/day) and prednisolone. After 6 weeks, he develops painless haematuria. Cystoscopy confirms cyclophosphamide-induced haemorrhagic cystitis. Which of the following drugs is now contraindicated?
questions.explanation: Cyclophosphamide is contraindicated in haemorrhagic cystitis due to its urotoxic metabolite acrolein; it should be discontinued. Mesna is used for prophylaxis, not contraindicated; other options are safe alternatives.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 12 weeks gestation presents with haemoptysis and haematuria. Anti-GBM antibodies are positive. She has normal renal function. Which of the following therapies is contraindicated in her pregnancy?
questions.explanation: Cyclophosphamide is teratogenic, especially in the first trimester, and is contraindicated in pregnancy. Azathioprine is relatively safer; plasma exchange and corticosteroids are not contraindicated.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with anti-GBM disease was treated with cyclophosphamide and corticosteroids for 3 months. He is now in clinical remission, anti-GBM antibodies are negative, and renal function is stable (creatinine 120 μmol/L). Which of the following is the most appropriate management for long-term maintenance?
options: Continue oral cyclophosphamide for a total of 6 months
questions.explanation: Anti-GBM disease is typically monophasic; after induction of remission and antibody clearance, maintenance immunosuppression is not routinely required—only careful monitoring for relapse. Continued cyclophosphamide risks toxicity; other agents are unnecessary.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man presents with haemoptysis and frank haematuria. Urgent renal biopsy shows crescents with linear IgG staining along the glomerular basement membrane, and anti-GBM antibodies are positive. His creatinine is 400 µmol/L, oxygen saturations 92% on air. What is the most appropriate immediate next step in management?
options: Perform urgent bronchoscopy to confirm alveolar haemorrhage
Insert a central line for urgent dialysis
Start high-dose oral prednisolone and cyclophosphamide
Commence intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days
questions.explanation: Plasma exchange rapidly removes pathogenic anti-GBM antibodies and is the cornerstone of immediate therapy in severe disease with pulmonary involvement. While immunosuppression with corticosteroids and cyclophosphamide is essential, plasma exchange should be started first alongside or immediately after starting immunosuppression. Dialysis may be needed later but is not the immediate priority; bronchoscopy is unnecessary for diagnosis given positive serology and biopsy.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman is found to have anti-GBM antibodies on screening after presenting with microscopic haematuria and proteinuria. Her creatinine is 120 µmol/L, eGFR >60, she has no respiratory symptoms, and a chest X-ray is clear. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Monitor renal function and repeat anti-GBM titre in 3 months
Perform a renal biopsy to guide treatment
questions.explanation: Even with mild disease, renal biopsy is indicated to assess the degree of activity and chronicity, which guides treatment intensity. Treatment is warranted to prevent progression, but the biopsy provides essential prognostic information. Monitoring alone risks irreversible damage; immunosuppression without histology may be inappropriate if there is significant chronicity. ACE inhibitors are not first-line.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with known anti-GBM disease on immunosuppression presents to the emergency department with massive haemoptysis and respiratory distress. His oxygen saturations are 78% on a non-rebreather mask. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Give a stat dose of intravenous frusemide
questions.explanation: In life-threatening haemoptysis with severe hypoxia, securing the airway and providing ventilatory support is the immediate priority to prevent death from respiratory failure. Plasma exchange and immunosuppression are definitive treatments but cannot be performed until the airway is safe. Frusemide is inappropriate for pulmonary haemorrhage.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with anti-GBM disease on oral prednisolone and cyclophosphamide develops a temperature of 38.9°C and her neutrophil count is 0.4 x 10⁹/L. She is haemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Take blood cultures and await results before treating
Arrange an urgent CT chest to look for a source of infection
Stop cyclophosphamide and start empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics
questions.explanation: Febrile neutropenia is a medical emergency. Immediate cessation of the offending agent (cyclophosphamide) and prompt initiation of empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics per local protocol is guidelines-based. G-CSF may be considered adjunctively but not first; awaiting cultures or imaging delays life-saving treatment.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman with a history of anti-GBM disease successfully treated 2 years ago now presents at 8 weeks pregnant. She is asymptomatic, urine dipstick normal, and current anti-GBM titre is negative. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer for joint obstetric-rheumatology specialist review
Check anti-GBM titre monthly throughout pregnancy
Reassure and arrange routine antenatal care
Start low-dose prednisolone as prophylaxis against relapse
Plan for early delivery to avoid disease flare
questions.explanation: Given the risk of relapse during pregnancy, multidisciplinary management by obstetric and rheumatology specialists is essential for monitoring and planning. Prophylactic steroids are not indicated without evidence of activity; monthly titres are part of the shared care plan but not the immediate next step. Reassurance alone is insufficient, and early delivery is not needed.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old male presents with haemoptysis, haematuria, and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. He is in respiratory distress. Which clinical finding indicates life-threatening pulmonary haemorrhage requiring immediate escalation to intensive care?
questions.explanation: In Goodpasture syndrome, diffuse alveolar haemorrhage can cause rapid desaturation. Oxygen saturation below 90% on room air signifies severe respiratory compromise and necessitates ICU admission. Frank haemoptysis is not always present; hypoxia is the most objective marker.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with known anti-GBM disease on maintenance prednisolone and mycophenolate attends for routine review. She feels well. Which finding is the earliest red flag of disease relapse?
questions.explanation: In anti-GBM disease, recurrent glomerulonephritis typically first manifests as haematuria, often before a rise in creatinine or antibody titres. Microscopic haematuria is the most sensitive early sign of active renal involvement.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old male with known anti-GBM disease on treatment presents with acute-onset dyspnoea and haemoptysis. His oxygen saturation is 91% on room air. Which laboratory finding is most indicative of life-threatening diffuse alveolar haemorrhage?
options: Haemoglobin drop of 30 g/L from baseline
questions.explanation: Acute haemoptysis with a significant drop in haemoglobin points to ongoing pulmonary bleeding. In diffuse alveolar haemorrhage, the fall in Hb is a direct measure of blood loss and severity, whereas inflammatory markers are non-specific. D-dimer may be elevated in PE, but Hb drop with pulmonary infiltrates is characteristic of alveolar haemorrhage.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old male with newly diagnosed anti-GBM disease is oliguric. His blood pressure is 150/90 mmHg. Which laboratory result requires immediate referral for haemodialysis?
questions.explanation: Hyperkalaemia is a medical emergency in acute kidney injury. Life-threatening arrhythmias can occur. While high creatinine and uraemia indicate severe renal impairment, the immediate danger is hyperkalaemia, which requires urgent dialysis. pH and bicarbonate indicate metabolic acidosis but not as acutely dangerous as hyperkalaemia.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with anti-GBM disease in remission on low-dose prednisolone and mycophenolate presents with a 3-day history of fever, non-productive cough, and pleuritic chest pain. Her oxygen saturation is 94% on room air. Which chest X-ray finding is most concerning for a life-threatening opportunistic infection?
options: Cavitating lesion in the right upper lobe
questions.explanation: In immunocompromised patients, bilateral interstitial infiltrates with fever and cough are classic for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP), a life-threatening infection. This pattern warrants urgent diagnostic bronchoscopy and empirical treatment. Lobar consolidation suggests typical bacterial pneumonia; cavitation suggests fungal or mycobacterial infection but is less common in this context. A normal CXR does not exclude early PCP, but abnormal with bilateral pattern is most concerning.
